title: Changing Women in a Changing Society at 50: A Symposium
abstract: We convened this symposium to celebrate the 50th anniversary of a special issue of the American Journal of Sociology ( AJS ): “ Changing Women in a ChangingSociety ” (hereafter “ ChangingWomen ” ).Across21articles, “ Changing Women ” featured 19 women as solo or ﬁ rst authors at a time when readers of the AJS might encounter only 50 woman-authored papers in a decade (Hughes 1975). Editor Joan Huber ’ s introduction situated the pressing need for the volume in sociology, noting the marginalization of women ’ s participation in the discipline and the glaring omission of research about women ’ s lives in the pages of sociology journals. The articles included critical interventions into mainstream sociological theory, demographic analyses about women ’ s health, statistical research about women ’ s workplace outcomes, empirical studies of women ’ s lives, and political autobiographies. The issue tapped into the zeitgeist of feminist activism, the increased political participation of women at the state and national levels, and the wave of gender eq-uity policies passed in the early 1970s in the United States. In 1972, Title IX, the federal civil rights law prohibiting sex-based discrimination in education, had been signed into law and the Equal Rights Amendment was submitted to state legislatures for rati ﬁ cation. Representative Shirley Chisholm became the ﬁ rst Black person and the ﬁ rst woman to seek the Democratic presidential nomination. And just as “ Changing Women ” was rolling off the presses, the landmark Supreme Court case Roe v. Wade (1973) struck down state antiabortion laws. It is bittersweet to revisit this
